g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Methods for Handling Deadlocks in Operating Systems.
A deadlock occurs when processes permanently block each other while competing for system resources.
g.text: A deadlock is a permanent blocking of processes that compete for finite resources.
Each process waits for an event that only another process in the set can cause.

g.data-narration: For a deadlock to occur, four conditions must hold simultaneously: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, and Circular Wait.
There are three main strategies to handle deadlocks: Prevention, Avoidance, and Detection with Recovery.
Deadlock Prevention ensures at least one of the four conditions cannot hold, guaranteeing no deadlocks can occur.
g.text: Design system to ensure at least one necessary condition CANNOT hold.

g.data-narration: Key prevention techniques include requesting all resources upfront, forcing resource release, and ordering resource requests.
g.text: Request ALL resources before execution starts
Impose resource ordering: R1 < R2 < R3...
Example: Scanner(R1) < Printer(R2) < Tape(R3) - request in increasing order breaks circular dependency

g.data-narration: Deadlock Avoidance dynamically checks if granting a request could lead to an unsafe state before allocation.
g.text: Requires a priori knowledge of maximum resource needs. System checks safety before granting.
✓ Safe State: A sequence exists where all processes complete
✗ Unsafe State: No safe sequence exists - deadlock possible

g.data-narration: The Banker's Algorithm is the most common deadlock avoidance method, simulating allocations to check system safety.
g.text: For each resource request, simulate allocation and check if resulting state remains safe.
Only grant if safe - otherwise delay or deny the request.

g.data-narration: Banker's Algorithm uses three key data structures: Max resources needed, Currently Allocated, and Available resources.
g.text: Maximum resources each process may need
Currently allocated resources to each process
Free resources currently available in system

g.data-narration: Example: With 10 printers, if P1 needs 8 total and has 4, the OS ensures enough remain for others before granting more.
Deadlock Detection allows the system to enter deadlock state, then periodically runs algorithms to detect and identify deadlocked processes.
g.text: Allow deadlock to occur, then detect periodically. If detected, recover using termination or preemption.
Detection: Check resource allocation graph for cycles

g.data-narration: One recovery method is process termination: either abort all deadlocked processes or abort one at a time until cycle breaks.
g.text: Simple but drastic - loses all work
Abort One Process at a Time

g.data-narration: Resource preemption forcibly takes resources from deadlocked processes, but requires careful victim selection and rollback mechanisms.
g.text: Preempt resources from deadlocked processes and allocate to others until deadlock breaks.

g.data-narration: Let's compare the three methods: Prevention guarantees no deadlocks but has low utilization; Avoidance balances safety and efficiency; Detection allows maximum utilization but with recovery costs.
Most OSes like Windows and Linux use the Ostrich Algorithm - they ignore deadlock for most resources, relying on simple techniques instead.
g.text: Most OSes (Windows, Linux) ignore deadlock for most resources - cost exceeds rare occurrence!
Used for: CPU, memory locks | Not used for: printers, tape drives in critical systems
